request.question: What is the title of the work, based on Philippa Gregory's historical novel series "The Cousins' War" did Max Irons take part?

title: The Kingmaker's Daughter
The White Queen (novel)
The King's Curse
The Women of the Cousins' War
The White Princess
The Queen's Fool
The White Queen (TV series)
The Red Queen (novel)
Max Irons
The Lady of the Rivers
sentences: The Kingmaker's Daughter is a 2012 historical novel by Philippa Gregory, part of her series "The Cousins' War".
It is the story of Anne Neville, wife of Richard III of England.

The 2013 BBC One television series "The White Queen" is a 10-part adaptation of Gregory's novels "The White Queen" (2009), "The Red Queen" (2010) and "The Kingmaker's Daughter", and features Faye Marsay as Anne Neville.
The White Queen is a 2009 historical novel by Philippa Gregory, the first of her series "The Cousins' War".
It tells the story of Elizabeth Woodville, queen consort of King Edward IV of England.

The 2013 BBC One television series "The White Queen" is a 10-part adaptation of Gregory's novels "The White Queen", "The Red Queen" (2010) and "The Kingmaker's Daughter" (2012), and features Rebecca Ferguson as Elizabeth Woodville.
The King's Curse is a 2014 historical novel by Philippa Gregory, part of her series "The Cousins' War".
A direct sequel to "The White Princess", it follows the adult life of Margaret Pole, Countess of Salisbury, the daughter of George Plantagenet, 1st Duke of Clarence and Isabel Neville.
The Women of the Cousins' War: The Duchess, the Queen and the King's Mother is a 2011 book by historical novelist Philippa Gregory and historians David Baldwin and Michael Jones.
It is a non-fiction work that explores the lives of three prominent women of the historical Wars of the Roses, all of whom Gregory has featured in her "Cousins' War" series of novels.
The White Princess is a 2013 historical novel by Philippa Gregory, part of her series "The Cousins' War".
It is the story of Elizabeth of York, daughter of Edward IV of England and Elizabeth Woodville, and later wife of Henry VII and mother of Henry VIII.
The Queen's Fool by Philippa Gregory is a 2004 historical fiction novel.
Set between 1548 and 1558, it is part of Philippa Gregory's Tudor series.
The series includes "The Boleyn Inheritance".

The novel chronicles the changing fortunes of Mary I of England and her half-sister Elizabeth through the eyes of the fictional Hannah Green, a Marrano girl escaping to England from Spain where her mother was burned at the stake for being Jewish.
Hannah is discovered by Robert Dudley and John Dee and subsequently begged as a fool to Edward VI.
She witnesses and becomes caught up the intrigues of the young king's court, and later those of his sisters.
As Mary, Elizabeth, and Robert Dudley use Hannah to gather information on their rivals and further their own aims, the novel can plausibly present each side in the complex story.
"The Queen's Fool" follows Hannah from ages fourteen to nineteen, and her coming-of-age is interspersed among the historical narrative (see Bildungsroman).
The book reached # 29 on the "New York Times" Best Seller list and had sold 165,000 copies within three weeks of its release.

The White Queen is a British television drama series in ten parts, based on Philippa Gregory's historical novel series "The Cousins' War" ("The White Queen", "The Red Queen", and "The Kingmaker's Daughter").
The first episode was premiered on BBC One on 16 June 2013 in the United Kingdom.
It was first broadcast in the United States on Starz on 9 August 2013.
The Red Queen is a 2010 historical novel by Philippa Gregory, the second of her series "The Cousins' War".
It is the story of Margaret Beaufort, mother of Henry VII of England.

The 2013 BBC One television series "The White Queen" is a 10-part adaptation of Gregory's novels "The White Queen" (2009), "The Red Queen" and "The Kingmaker's Daughter" (2012), and features Amanda Hale as Margaret Beaufort.
Maximilian Paul Diarmuid "Max" Irons (born 17 October 1985) is an English-Irish actor and model.
He is known for his roles in "Red Riding Hood" (2011), "The White Queen" (2013), "The Host" (2013), and "The Riot Club" (2014).
The Lady of the Rivers is a 2011 historical novel by Philippa Gregory, part of her series "The Cousins' War".
The story is narrated by Jacquetta of Luxembourg, mother of Elizabeth Woodville, and covers the reign of the Lancastrian King Henry VI.
The novel serves as a prequel to Gregory's "The White Queen" (2009), the story of Elizabeth's reign as Queen of England.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

response.summary_1:
Philippa Gregory's "Cousins' War" novels and the TV adaptation "The White Queen".

- "The White Queen (TV series)" — "a British television drama series in ten parts, based on Philippa Gregory's historical novel series 'The Cousins' War' ('The White Queen', 'The Red Queen', and 'The Kingmaker's Daughter')."
- Premiere/broadcast dates: "premiered on BBC One on 16 June 2013" (UK) and "first broadcast in the United States on Starz on 9 August 2013."
- Source novels and years: "The White Queen" (2009) — story of "Elizabeth Woodville, queen consort of King Edward IV of England"; "The Red Queen" (2010) — story of "Margaret Beaufort"; "The Kingmaker's Daughter" (2012) — story of "Anne Neville" (the adaptation "features Faye Marsay as Anne Neville").
- Missing fact (blocks answering): Whether "Max Irons took part" and, if so, which production/role — NOT stated in the provided passages. Bridge entity for next hop: "Max Irons" ⇄ "The White Queen (TV series)".
- Next-hop retrieval directives (priority order):
  1. Fetch the "Max Irons" Wikipedia page — inspect the lede and "Filmography" / "Television" sections for credits (look for 2013 credits).
  2. Fetch "The White Queen (TV series)" page — inspect the "Cast" or infobox/cast list section.
  3. Search query: "Max Irons The White Queen cast Edward IV 2013" (or "Max Irons White Queen role").
  4. If needed, fetch "Max Irons filmography" subsection or reputable cast lists (BBC/Starz press pages).
response.hop2_query: Max Irons The White Queen cast role
hop2_docs: Max Irons | Maximilian Paul Diarmuid "Max" Irons (born 17 October 1985) is an English-Irish actor and model.  He is known for his roles in "Red Riding Hood" (2011), "The White Queen" (2013), "The Host" (2013), and "The Riot Club" (2014).

Cast-iron architecture | Cast-iron architecture is a form of architecture where cast iron plays a central role.  It was a prominent style in the Industrial Revolution era when cast iron was relatively cheap and modern steel had not yet been developed.

Cast iron | Cast iron is a group of iron-carbon alloys with a carbon content greater than 2%.  Its usefulness derives from its relatively low melting temperature.  The alloy constituents affect its colour when fractured: white cast iron has carbide impurities which allow cracks to pass straight through, grey cast iron has graphite flakes which deflect a passing crack and initiate countless new cracks as the material breaks, and ductile cast iron has spherical graphite "nodules" which stop the crack from further progressing.

Malleable iron | Malleable iron is cast as white iron, the structure being a metastable carbide in a pearlitic matrix.  Through an annealing heat treatment, the brittle structure as first cast is transformed into the malleable form.  Carbon agglomerates into small roughly spherical aggregates of graphite leaving a matrix of ferrite or pearlite according to the exact heat treatment used.  Three basic types of malleable iron are recognized within the casting industry: "blackheart" malleable iron, "whiteheart" malleable iron and "pearlitic" malleable iron.

Jessica Stroup | Jessica Leigh Stroup (born October 23, 1986) is an American actress, best known for her role as Erin Silver on "90210", Max Hardy on "The Following", and Joy Meachum in "Iron Fist".  She is regarded as a scream queen for starring in the horror films "Prom Night", "Vampire Bats", "Left in Darkness", and "The Hills Have Eyes 2".

Whitewashing in film | Whitewashing is a casting practice in the film industry of the United States in which white actors are cast in historically non-white character roles.  The film industry has a history of frequently casting white actors for roles involving people of color, including African Americans, Asians, Hispanics, and any other non-White, non-Hispanic culture, including Native Americans.  By downplaying the roles that such figures have had in cultural events, the practice is seen as a form of censorship analogous to the whitewashing of criticism.

Ductile iron | Ductile iron, also known as ductile cast iron, nodular cast iron, spheroidal graphite iron, spheroidal graphite cast iron and SG iron, is a type of graphite-rich cast iron discovered in 1943 by Keith Millis.  While most varieties of cast iron are brittle, ductile iron has much more impact and fatigue resistance, due to its "nodular graphite" inclusions.
response.summary_2: Max Irons took part in The White Queen. Max Irons is an English-Irish actor known for his role in the 2013 TV adaptation The White Queen.
